steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content:
We have two complete thoughts that need proper punctuation between them:
“Okinaka doesn't make such decisions single-handedly” (independent clause)
“All historical designations must be approved by a group of nine other experts” (independent clause)
The word “however” signals a contrast between them.

steps.title: Apply the SAT rule for conjunctive adverbs
steps.content:
When a conjunctive adverb like “however” joins two independent clauses, the SAT standard patterns are: IC; however, IC (semicolon before, comma after) or IC.

However, IC (period before, starting a new sentence).

Choice D shows the second pattern—the period after “however” ends the first sentence, and the capital “A” in “All” begins the second.

The other choices fail because they lack proper punctuation before “however” (A and C both have only a comma before it, which violates the rule) or add an irrelevant word (B).

That matches choice D — D is correct.
